question: can you sell vegetables from your home garden
title: Can You Sell Vegetables From Your Home Garden? Yes—And Here's How to Start
metaDescription: Scotty walked into his first farmers market selling backyard vegetables from Houston, Texas. Here's what he learned about selling produce legally, practically, and profitably.
content:
# Can You Sell Vegetables From Your Home Garden? Yes—And Here's How to Start

I remember standing in my backyard in the middle of Houston with sixteen raised beds of cucumbers, tomatoes going crazy, and more baba ganoush than any one human family could ever eat. And I thought—somebody out there would pay for this. Not because I needed the money, but because food this good deserves to be eaten by people who appreciate it. And because the whole point of building a living, productive garden is to share what it produces.

So I did it. I loaded up my truck, set up a booth, and sold garden vegetables at the farmers market. And let me tell you—it is one of the most satisfying things I have ever done in my life. But before you do it, there are a few things you need to know.

## Yes, You Can Legally Sell Vegetables in Texas

Let's get the legal question out of the way first, because this is where most people get scared off and they shouldn't be. If you're in Texas—and honestly in most states—you can absolutely sell fresh, unprocessed vegetables directly to consumers. You don't need a commercial kitchen. You don't need a food handlers permit for raw produce. You don't need to register with a state food agency.

The Texas Cottage Food Law covers home-produced food products sold directly to consumers. Raw and uncut vegetables fall under the most permissive category—they are not time-and-temperature-controlled foods, which means the regulatory burden is essentially zero for fresh produce. You can sell at farmers markets, roadside stands, community events, and directly to neighbors.

Now, if you want to sell processed items—pickled cucumbers, fermented peppers, dehydrated tomatoes—that gets a little bit more complicated. Those still fall under cottage food in most cases, but you do need to have completed an accredited basic food safety course, and there are labeling requirements. The income threshold for cottage food is currently up to $150,000 annually, which is a lot of salsa.

I sold fermented foods to Whole Foods and Central Market for years before I pivoted to selling fresh garden produce. The regulatory path for fermented products required more documentation, but it was absolutely doable. For fresh vegetables, though? It's wide open.

## Start Smaller Than You Think You Need To

Here's what I wish someone had told me before my first farmers market: you don't need to grow enough to fill a produce aisle. You need to grow enough to fill a table. And you need that table to look abundant and intentional.

I was nervous going into my first market that I wouldn't have enough to sell. I had twelve cases of fermented cucumber salad, a good amount of baba ganoush, some peppers and onions, and fresh tomatoes starting to come in. Seemed thin to me at the time. But presentation matters enormously. When you grow things yourself, with living soil and real compost, the product looks different. It tastes different. People can tell.

My goal for my first market was to break $500. I did not hit that target. But I came close enough to know I was onto something real. By market four, I had worked out the kinks—what to bring, how to display it, how to talk about what made my soil different from conventional growing.

## The Soil Is Your Competitive Advantage

This is the thing that nobody tells you when they talk about backyard market gardening. Your price and your quality justification is not just "I grew this." It's *how* you grew this.

I grow in raised beds with compost-only soil. No synthetic fertilizers, no herbicides, no pesticides. My cucumber beds are fed by restaurant kitchen scraps and wood chips that I source from local tree services. The whole system runs on the decay cycle—organic matter breaks down into humus, humus feeds the microbe community, microbes feed the plants, plants feed the people. That is the complete biological loop that Albert Howard spent his whole career writing about.

Gabe Brown talks about this in *Dirt to Soil*—the idea that once you stop disrupting the soil biology with synthetic inputs, the microbial communities begin to recover and create something genuinely different. My cucumbers go directly from that living soil into fermented products where those living microbes keep doing their work. That's a story worth telling at a market booth.

When you're selling to somebody at a farmers market, you're not competing with grocery stores on price. You're competing on story, on quality, on relationship. That's a fight a backyard grower can win.

## What Actually Sells at Farmers Markets

Fresh vegetables move well when they look beautiful and they're in season. Here's what I've found works at Houston-area markets:

Tomatoes. Everybody wants tomatoes, especially anything heirloom or interesting looking. Cucumbers sell fast in the right season. Peppers, especially if you have variety. Herbs do well—basil, cilantro, anything fragrant and fresh. Leafy greens in the cooler months are strong sellers.

Value-added products—jams, fermented vegetables, dried herbs, sauces—those command significantly higher price points. My fermented products sell at a premium because people understand they're getting something alive, something that grocery stores literally cannot stock in the same way.

I also sell the story of the garden itself. People want to know how I grow. They want to know about the compost, the raised beds, the chickens, the whole system. That conversation is free, and it builds loyalty that keeps people coming back every single Saturday.

## The First Step Is Simpler Than You Think

If you're sitting on a productive garden right now and wondering whether this is possible—it is. The barrier to entry for selling fresh produce at a farmers market in Texas is as low as it gets in any business. You need the product, a table, and a way to talk about what makes yours different.

Find your local farmers market, call them up, and ask about vendor applications. Most markets have a waiting list, but smaller neighborhood markets often have openings. Show up as a customer first. Walk the market, talk to vendors, understand the culture. Farmers markets are communities. They run on relationships, same as the soil biology they're supposed to celebrate.

I started this whole thing because I believe that food and water sufficiency at the community level is the first step toward real independence. When every backyard is a productive garden, when every neighborhood has a farmers market, when people know where their food comes from and who grew it—that's when something real shifts. And it all starts with somebody saying yes, I'm going to sell these vegetables.

## Sources

- [Texas Cottage Food Production - Texas DSHS](https://www.dshs.texas.gov/retail-food-establishments/texas-cottage-food-production)
- [FAQ - Texas Cottage Food Law](https://texascottagefoodlaw.com/faq/)
- [Selling Foods Made at Home: Texas Cottage Food Law - Texas A&M AgriLife Extension](https://agrilifeextension.tamu.edu/wp-content/uploads/2025/08/Selling-Food-at-Home-Texas-Cottage-Food-Law.pdf)
- [Farmers Markets as Community Centerpieces - USDA](https://www.usda.gov/media/blog/2013/08/05/farmers-markets-community-centerpieces)
